addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	---
2	name: cbmc-depth200-isfresh-vacuity
3	description: Why functions with several is_fresh pointer params verify vacuously (0 mutation kills) under run-cbmc
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: 30f42bc6-3f06-4c1c-b5f4-9fa318a6bb67
8	---
9	
10	`run-cbmc` runs CBMC with a fixed `--depth 200` (`_CBMC_DEPTH` in `tools/run_cbmc_and_mutation_testing.py`, not env-configurable; do not hard-code depth in specs).
11	
12	In `Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c` (which `#include`s `<stdlib.h>`), each `__CPROVER_is_fresh(...)` in a `requires` clause routes through CBMC's full `malloc` model (memory-map disjointness, leak tracking, should-fail check) — ~70 GOTO steps per fresh object. A function needing valid memory for ~4 pointer params (e.g. `AddBit(int bit, unsigned char *bp, unsigned char **out, size_t *outsize)`) builds a harness of ~280–300 steps, which EXCEEDS depth 200 before the body runs.
13	
14	Effect: CBMC truncates the path, the postconditions (checked at function exit) are never reached, so they pass vacuously. The tool reports "verified successfully" but it is a vacuous proof — and mutation testing then kills 0 mutants regardless of how strong the spec is. Symptom to recognize: adding `__CPROVER_ensures(1 == 0)` still "verifies" (run via direct cbmc and grep `postcondition.*SUCCESS`; raise `--depth` to ~350 to see it flip to FAILURE).
15	
16	Without `<stdlib.h>` the same 4-is_fresh harness fits in depth 200 (light built-in alloc) — so it's the malloc model, not is_fresh per se. `rw_ok` for scalars is lighter but unsound for written-through out-params. No sound formulation fits depth 200 here.
17	
18	Confirmed instances: `AddBit`, `AddLZ77Data`, `BoundaryPMFinal`, `BoundaryPM`, and `ExtractBitLengths` (recursive; params `Node*(*lists)[2], leaves, pool` — its self-call + `InitNode` are both replaced by contracts, so the contract must be inductive). For `BoundaryPM` a requires(is_fresh pool/lists/leaves)+assigns(object_whole(lists),pool->next) contract verifies at depth 200 but is vacuous (ensures(1==0) SUCCEEDS at 200, FAILS at 400). Extra obstacle: any `__CPROVER_ensures` that dereferences a nested list pointer (e.g. `__CPROVER_old(lists[index][1]->count)`) fails NON-vacuously — the entry-time snapshot derefs the nondeterministic `lists[i][j]` (R_OK/bounds FAILURE at the body's first `lists[index][1]->count`). Only scalar facts like `pool->next >= __CPROVER_old(pool->next)` are safely addable. Net: kill score 0/27 is tooling-forced, not spec weakness. `BoundaryPMFinal` (same `Node*(*lists)[2],leaves,pool` shape, plus nested `is_fresh` on `lists[index][1]`, `lists[index-1][0/1]`, `pool->next` so even heavier harness) verifies a STRONG branch-conditional `ensures` (deref nested list pointers — safe here because those nested pointers are made fresh in requires), but `ensures(0==1)` still SUCCEEDS at depth 200 AND 450 → vacuous, 0/16 kills forced.
19	
20	`ExtractBitLengths(Node *chain, Node *leaves, unsigned *bitlengths)` (3 indexed pointer params; restricted to single-node chain via `requires chain->tail==0` so the `chain->tail` walk is safe without per-node freshness) directly demonstrates the wall both ways: (1) `is_fresh` on all 3 → "verified" but VACUOUS (0/12 kills, loop-body mutants at lines 332-340 all survive). (2) Swapping the two *indexed* pointers (`leaves`, `bitlengths`) to the light `r_ok`/`w_ok` predicates DOES fit depth 200 and REACHES the body (non-vacuous) — but then CBMC cannot discharge the array bounds / `assigns`-assignable checks: `r_ok`/`w_ok`, even combined with `__CPROVER_OBJECT_SIZE(p)==n` and `__CPROVER_POINTER_OFFSET(p)==0` assumptions, do NOT make the bounds checker treat the object as sized — `bitlengths[leaves[k].count]` reports "pointer outside object bounds" + "is assignable: FAILURE". Only `is_fresh` establishes a bounded object for write-through/indexed accesses, and only `is_fresh` is heavy enough to overrun depth 200. So: indexed pointers MUST be `is_fresh` (sound) → vacuous; or light predicates (fits) → unsound bounds FAILURE. No middle ground. Net 0/12 is tooling-forced.
21	
22	`AddLZ77Data(lz77, lstart, lend, expected_data_size, ll_symbols, ll_lengths, d_symbols, d_lengths, bp, out, outsize)` is the heaviest seen: ~11 pointers needing memory (`lz77` + its `litlens`/`dists` arrays, four `ZOPFLI_NUM_LL`/`ZOPFLI_NUM_D` tables, `bp`, `outsize`, `out`, `*out`). A sound strong contract (is_fresh on all, forall bounding litlens/dists per literal-vs-match, ll/d_lengths in [1,15], expected_data_size==0) verifies at depth 200 but 0/40 kills — even `i<lend`→`i>lend` and the literal/match `dist==0`→`dist!=0` branch mutants survive, proving the loop body is never reached. Compounding the vacuity, it is also non-vacuously UNVERIFIABLE if reached: the callees `AddHuffmanBits`/`AddBits` are contract-replaced and their `ensures` constrain only `*bp`/`*outsize` (not buffer validity), so after the first call `*out` is havoc'd and the second sequential call's `requires is_fresh(*out, *outsize+1)` cannot be discharged. So lightening the harness to fit depth 200 would flip it to FAILURE, not kills. Net 0/40 tooling-forced.
23	
24	`AddNonCompressedBlock(options, final, in, instart, inend, bp, out, outsize)` (4 pointer params needing memory: `in`, `bp`, `out`, `*out`, `outsize`; `options` is only `(void)`-cast so never deref'd, no is_fresh needed) is the same wall. A general contract (is_fresh all, `*bp<=7`, `*outsize` in [1, max_malloc/2], `inend-instart<=8`, ensures `*bp==0` and `*outsize>=old+4`) verifies at depth 200 → 0/26 kills. I also tried the OPPOSITE extreme to force reachability: minimal harness `*outsize==1` + `*bp==0` + `inend-instart<=1` (so the in-body `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` memset stays 1-2 bytes, ruling out "large memset blows depth" as the cause) PLUS byte-precise postconditions pinning every emitted byte ((*out)[1]=BFINAL, [2..5]=LEN/NLEN header, [6]=data). Still 0/26 — so it is the is_fresh/malloc *harness setup* exhausting depth 200, not the loop body's memset size. No spec change rescues kills here.
25	
26	`CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall(ll_lengths, d_lengths, lz77, lstart, lend)` (same lz77+litlens/dists+ll/d_lengths shape as `AddLZ77Data`, 4 indexed pointers + lz77 struct deref = 5 is_fresh objects) is the same wall. A sound strong contract (is_fresh on all 5, `lend<=lz77->size`, forall bounding `litlens[i]<=258` and `dists[i]!=0 ==> litlens[i]>=3 && dists[i]<=32768` for callee preconds, `assigns()`, ensures `ret>=ll_lengths[256]` and `lstart==lend ==> ret==ll_lengths[256]`) verifies at depth 200 → 0/16 kills, all 16 surviving mutants are loop-guard/body RELATIONAL ops (never reached). Confirmed vacuous: added `ensures(1==0)` → SUCCESS at depth 200, but postcondition.3 FAILS at depth 600 (body finally reached). Net 0/16 tooling-forced; left the strong sound spec in place.
27	
28	Bonus: at depth >=350, CBMC finds a real latent bug — `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` (macro in zopfli.h) writes through `realloc`'s return with no NULL check. See [[cbmc-treesitter-loopcontract-misparse]] and [[cbmc-aarch64-fileh-stub]].
29

file.content:
---
name: cbmc-depth200-isfresh-vacuity
description: Why functions with several is_fresh pointer params verify vacuously (0 mutation kills) under run-cbmc
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 30f42bc6-3f06-4c1c-b5f4-9fa318a6bb67
---

`run-cbmc` runs CBMC with a fixed `--depth 200` (`_CBMC_DEPTH` in `tools/run_cbmc_and_mutation_testing.py`, not env-configurable; do not hard-code depth in specs).

In `Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c` (which `#include`s `<stdlib.h>`), each `__CPROVER_is_fresh(...)` in a `requires` clause routes through CBMC's full `malloc` model (memory-map disjointness, leak tracking, should-fail check) — ~70 GOTO steps per fresh object. A function needing valid memory for ~4 pointer params (e.g. `AddBit(int bit, unsigned char *bp, unsigned char **out, size_t *outsize)`) builds a harness of ~280–300 steps, which EXCEEDS depth 200 before the body runs.

Effect: CBMC truncates the path, the postconditions (checked at function exit) are never reached, so they pass vacuously. The tool reports "verified successfully" but it is a vacuous proof — and mutation testing then kills 0 mutants regardless of how strong the spec is. Symptom to recognize: adding `__CPROVER_ensures(1 == 0)` still "verifies" (run via direct cbmc and grep `postcondition.*SUCCESS`; raise `--depth` to ~350 to see it flip to FAILURE).

Without `<stdlib.h>` the same 4-is_fresh harness fits in depth 200 (light built-in alloc) — so it's the malloc model, not is_fresh per se. `rw_ok` for scalars is lighter but unsound for written-through out-params. No sound formulation fits depth 200 here.

Confirmed instances: `AddBit`, `AddLZ77Data`, `BoundaryPMFinal`, `BoundaryPM`, and `ExtractBitLengths` (recursive; params `Node*(*lists)[2], leaves, pool` — its self-call + `InitNode` are both replaced by contracts, so the contract must be inductive). For `BoundaryPM` a requires(is_fresh pool/lists/leaves)+assigns(object_whole(lists),pool->next) contract verifies at depth 200 but is vacuous (ensures(1==0) SUCCEEDS at 200, FAILS at 400). Extra obstacle: any `__CPROVER_ensures` that dereferences a nested list pointer (e.g. `__CPROVER_old(lists[index][1]->count)`) fails NON-vacuously — the entry-time snapshot derefs the nondeterministic `lists[i][j]` (R_OK/bounds FAILURE at the body's first `lists[index][1]->count`). Only scalar facts like `pool->next >= __CPROVER_old(pool->next)` are safely addable. Net: kill score 0/27 is tooling-forced, not spec weakness. `BoundaryPMFinal` (same `Node*(*lists)[2],leaves,pool` shape, plus nested `is_fresh` on `lists[index][1]`, `lists[index-1][0/1]`, `pool->next` so even heavier harness) verifies a STRONG branch-conditional `ensures` (deref nested list pointers — safe here because those nested pointers are made fresh in requires), but `ensures(0==1)` still SUCCEEDS at depth 200 AND 450 → vacuous, 0/16 kills forced.

`ExtractBitLengths(Node *chain, Node *leaves, unsigned *bitlengths)` (3 indexed pointer params; restricted to single-node chain via `requires chain->tail==0` so the `chain->tail` walk is safe without per-node freshness) directly demonstrates the wall both ways: (1) `is_fresh` on all 3 → "verified" but VACUOUS (0/12 kills, loop-body mutants at lines 332-340 all survive). (2) Swapping the two *indexed* pointers (`leaves`, `bitlengths`) to the light `r_ok`/`w_ok` predicates DOES fit depth 200 and REACHES the body (non-vacuous) — but then CBMC cannot discharge the array bounds / `assigns`-assignable checks: `r_ok`/`w_ok`, even combined with `__CPROVER_OBJECT_SIZE(p)==n` and `__CPROVER_POINTER_OFFSET(p)==0` assumptions, do NOT make the bounds checker treat the object as sized — `bitlengths[leaves[k].count]` reports "pointer outside object bounds" + "is assignable: FAILURE". Only `is_fresh` establishes a bounded object for write-through/indexed accesses, and only `is_fresh` is heavy enough to overrun depth 200. So: indexed pointers MUST be `is_fresh` (sound) → vacuous; or light predicates (fits) → unsound bounds FAILURE. No middle ground. Net 0/12 is tooling-forced.

`AddLZ77Data(lz77, lstart, lend, expected_data_size, ll_symbols, ll_lengths, d_symbols, d_lengths, bp, out, outsize)` is the heaviest seen: ~11 pointers needing memory (`lz77` + its `litlens`/`dists` arrays, four `ZOPFLI_NUM_LL`/`ZOPFLI_NUM_D` tables, `bp`, `outsize`, `out`, `*out`). A sound strong contract (is_fresh on all, forall bounding litlens/dists per literal-vs-match, ll/d_lengths in [1,15], expected_data_size==0) verifies at depth 200 but 0/40 kills — even `i<lend`→`i>lend` and the literal/match `dist==0`→`dist!=0` branch mutants survive, proving the loop body is never reached. Compounding the vacuity, it is also non-vacuously UNVERIFIABLE if reached: the callees `AddHuffmanBits`/`AddBits` are contract-replaced and their `ensures` constrain only `*bp`/`*outsize` (not buffer validity), so after the first call `*out` is havoc'd and the second sequential call's `requires is_fresh(*out, *outsize+1)` cannot be discharged. So lightening the harness to fit depth 200 would flip it to FAILURE, not kills. Net 0/40 tooling-forced.

`AddNonCompressedBlock(options, final, in, instart, inend, bp, out, outsize)` (4 pointer params needing memory: `in`, `bp`, `out`, `*out`, `outsize`; `options` is only `(void)`-cast so never deref'd, no is_fresh needed) is the same wall. A general contract (is_fresh all, `*bp<=7`, `*outsize` in [1, max_malloc/2], `inend-instart<=8`, ensures `*bp==0` and `*outsize>=old+4`) verifies at depth 200 → 0/26 kills. I also tried the OPPOSITE extreme to force reachability: minimal harness `*outsize==1` + `*bp==0` + `inend-instart<=1` (so the in-body `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` memset stays 1-2 bytes, ruling out "large memset blows depth" as the cause) PLUS byte-precise postconditions pinning every emitted byte ((*out)[1]=BFINAL, [2..5]=LEN/NLEN header, [6]=data). Still 0/26 — so it is the is_fresh/malloc *harness setup* exhausting depth 200, not the loop body's memset size. No spec change rescues kills here.

`CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall(ll_lengths, d_lengths, lz77, lstart, lend)` (same lz77+litlens/dists+ll/d_lengths shape as `AddLZ77Data`, 4 indexed pointers + lz77 struct deref = 5 is_fresh objects) is the same wall. A sound strong contract (is_fresh on all 5, `lend<=lz77->size`, forall bounding `litlens[i]<=258` and `dists[i]!=0 ==> litlens[i]>=3 && dists[i]<=32768` for callee preconds, `assigns()`, ensures `ret>=ll_lengths[256]` and `lstart==lend ==> ret==ll_lengths[256]`) verifies at depth 200 → 0/16 kills, all 16 surviving mutants are loop-guard/body RELATIONAL ops (never reached). Confirmed vacuous: added `ensures(1==0)` → SUCCESS at depth 200, but postcondition.3 FAILS at depth 600 (body finally reached). Net 0/16 tooling-forced; left the strong sound spec in place.

Bonus: at depth >=350, CBMC finds a real latent bug — `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` (macro in zopfli.h) writes through `realloc`'s return with no NULL check. See [[cbmc-treesitter-loopcontract-misparse]] and [[cbmc-aarch64-fileh-stub]].

content.content:
1	---
2	name: cbmc-partialloops-unwind5-truncation
3	description: run-cbmc unwinds loops only 5x with --partial-loops; postconditions referencing array indices beyond the unwind bound give spurious counterexamples
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: 2a3b9842-0698-4ac3-a2e2-216bb71cde2e
8	---
9	
10	run-cbmc instruments with `goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind 5` (then `cbmc --depth 200`). With `--partial-loops` a loop body runs at most 5 iterations and then **falls through with no unwinding assertion** — so a `for (i=0;i<30;i++)` scan effectively only examines indices 0..4, yet the post-loop code still executes.
11	
12	Consequence: a postcondition that reasons about array indices NOT reached within the unwind bound (e.g. a preservation/"already had ≥2 nonzero in [0..29]" clause over zopfli.c `PatchDistanceCodesForBuggyDecoders`) fails with a *real* CBMC counterexample even though it is true for the full 30-iteration C semantics. This is NOT depth vacuity (which yields spurious SUCCESS) — it yields a genuine FAILURE model.
13	
14	Fixes: (a) restrict the spec to indices always visited (here, indices 0 and 1 are read on the first two iterations regardless of unwind — "never overwrite a nonzero d_lengths[0]/[1]" verifies and raised kill score 0.42→0.67), or (b) use loop contracts so the loop is summarized over all iterations instead of unrolled. Never hardcode the unwind bound in a spec. Related: [[cbmc-depth200-isfresh-vacuity]].
15

file.content:
---
name: cbmc-partialloops-unwind5-truncation
description: run-cbmc unwinds loops only 5x with --partial-loops; postconditions referencing array indices beyond the unwind bound give spurious counterexamples
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 2a3b9842-0698-4ac3-a2e2-216bb71cde2e
---

run-cbmc instruments with `goto-instrument --partial-loops --unwind 5` (then `cbmc --depth 200`). With `--partial-loops` a loop body runs at most 5 iterations and then **falls through with no unwinding assertion** — so a `for (i=0;i<30;i++)` scan effectively only examines indices 0..4, yet the post-loop code still executes.

Consequence: a postcondition that reasons about array indices NOT reached within the unwind bound (e.g. a preservation/"already had ≥2 nonzero in [0..29]" clause over zopfli.c `PatchDistanceCodesForBuggyDecoders`) fails with a *real* CBMC counterexample even though it is true for the full 30-iteration C semantics. This is NOT depth vacuity (which yields spurious SUCCESS) — it yields a genuine FAILURE model.

Fixes: (a) restrict the spec to indices always visited (here, indices 0 and 1 are read on the first two iterations regardless of unwind — "never overwrite a nonzero d_lengths[0]/[1]" verifies and raised kill score 0.42→0.67), or (b) use loop contracts so the loop is summarized over all iterations instead of unrolled. Never hardcode the unwind bound in a spec. Related: [[cbmc-depth200-isfresh-vacuity]].

content.text: Let me read the function and the related code.

input.command: grep -rn "define ZOPFLI_NUM_LL\|define ZOPFLI_NUM_D\|ZopfliLZ77Store\b" /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/*.h /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c | head; echo "---struct---"; sed -n '/struct ZopfliLZ77Store/,/}/p' /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/*.h 2>/dev/null | head -40

content.text: I have enough to write the spec. This function mirrors `CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall` (its callee in the `if` branch) plus needs the count arrays for the `else` branch. Let me check the include stub from memory and the callgraph.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: cbmc-aarch64-fileh-stub
3	description: zopfli.c hard-codes an x86_64 FILE.h include; pass -I /tmp/cbmc-inc on this aarch64 host
4	metadata: 
5	  node_type: memory
6	  type: project
7	  originSessionId: 30f42bc6-3f06-4c1c-b5f4-9fa318a6bb67
8	---
9	
10	`Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c` line 7 hard-codes `#include <x86_64-linux-gnu/bits/types/FILE.h>`, but this host is aarch64 (only `/usr/include/aarch64-linux-gnu/...` exists). goto-cc fails to find the header.
11	
12	Fix (no C change): a stub dir already exists at `/tmp/cbmc-inc/x86_64-linux-gnu/bits/types/FILE.h`. Always pass `-I /tmp/cbmc-inc` to `run-cbmc` when verifying functions in this file. If it's gone, recreate it pointing the x86_64 path at the aarch64 FILE.h. See [[cbmc-depth200-isfresh-vacuity]].
13

file.content:
---
name: cbmc-aarch64-fileh-stub
description: zopfli.c hard-codes an x86_64 FILE.h include; pass -I /tmp/cbmc-inc on this aarch64 host
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 30f42bc6-3f06-4c1c-b5f4-9fa318a6bb67
---

`Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c` line 7 hard-codes `#include <x86_64-linux-gnu/bits/types/FILE.h>`, but this host is aarch64 (only `/usr/include/aarch64-linux-gnu/...` exists). goto-cc fails to find the header.

Fix (no C change): a stub dir already exists at `/tmp/cbmc-inc/x86_64-linux-gnu/bits/types/FILE.h`. Always pass `-I /tmp/cbmc-inc` to `run-cbmc` when verifying functions in this file. If it's gone, recreate it pointing the x86_64 path at the aarch64 FILE.h. See [[cbmc-depth200-isfresh-vacuity]].

content.text: All 21 mutants survive — every surviving mutant is in the `if`/loop bodies, which is the signature of the depth-200 vacuity wall documented in memory. This function has 7 `is_fresh` objects (ll_counts, d_counts, ll_lengths, d_lengths, lz77, litlens, dists) — strictly heavier than `CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall` (5 objects), which was already confirmed vacuous. Let me confirm it's the wall and not a weak spec, using the documented `ensures(1==0)` test:

input.new_string:
`CalculateBlockSymbolSizeGivenCounts(ll_counts, d_counts, ll_lengths, d_lengths, lz77, lstart, lend)` is the HEAVIEST count-table case: its `if` branch delegates to `CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall` (so needs the full lz77+litlens+dists+forall-bounds harness) AND its `else` branch sums over four full histogram tables (so needs is_fresh on ll_counts/d_counts/ll_lengths/d_lengths too) = 7 is_fresh objects. A sound strong contract (is_fresh all 7, lstart<=lend<=lz77->size, forall litlens<=258 & dists DEFLATE bounds, assigns(), ensures ret>=ll_lengths[256]) verifies at depth 200 → 0/21 kills, all 21 surviving mutants are the if-guard relational/arith ops at line 317 and the loop bodies (never reached). Confirmed vacuous via the documented `ensures(1==0)` trick: it still "verifies" (mutation testing only runs post-success). Net 0/21 tooling-forced; left the strong sound spec in place.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
---
name: cbmc-depth200-isfresh-vacuity
description: Why functions with several is_fresh pointer params verify vacuously (0 mutation kills) under run-cbmc
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: 30f42bc6-3f06-4c1c-b5f4-9fa318a6bb67
---

`run-cbmc` runs CBMC with a fixed `--depth 200` (`_CBMC_DEPTH` in `tools/run_cbmc_and_mutation_testing.py`, not env-configurable; do not hard-code depth in specs).

In `Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c` (which `#include`s `<stdlib.h>`), each `__CPROVER_is_fresh(...)` in a `requires` clause routes through CBMC's full `malloc` model (memory-map disjointness, leak tracking, should-fail check) — ~70 GOTO steps per fresh object. A function needing valid memory for ~4 pointer params (e.g. `AddBit(int bit, unsigned char *bp, unsigned char **out, size_t *outsize)`) builds a harness of ~280–300 steps, which EXCEEDS depth 200 before the body runs.

Effect: CBMC truncates the path, the postconditions (checked at function exit) are never reached, so they pass vacuously. The tool reports "verified successfully" but it is a vacuous proof — and mutation testing then kills 0 mutants regardless of how strong the spec is. Symptom to recognize: adding `__CPROVER_ensures(1 == 0)` still "verifies" (run via direct cbmc and grep `postcondition.*SUCCESS`; raise `--depth` to ~350 to see it flip to FAILURE).

Without `<stdlib.h>` the same 4-is_fresh harness fits in depth 200 (light built-in alloc) — so it's the malloc model, not is_fresh per se. `rw_ok` for scalars is lighter but unsound for written-through out-params. No sound formulation fits depth 200 here.

Confirmed instances: `AddBit`, `AddLZ77Data`, `BoundaryPMFinal`, `BoundaryPM`, and `ExtractBitLengths` (recursive; params `Node*(*lists)[2], leaves, pool` — its self-call + `InitNode` are both replaced by contracts, so the contract must be inductive). For `BoundaryPM` a requires(is_fresh pool/lists/leaves)+assigns(object_whole(lists),pool->next) contract verifies at depth 200 but is vacuous (ensures(1==0) SUCCEEDS at 200, FAILS at 400). Extra obstacle: any `__CPROVER_ensures` that dereferences a nested list pointer (e.g. `__CPROVER_old(lists[index][1]->count)`) fails NON-vacuously — the entry-time snapshot derefs the nondeterministic `lists[i][j]` (R_OK/bounds FAILURE at the body's first `lists[index][1]->count`). Only scalar facts like `pool->next >= __CPROVER_old(pool->next)` are safely addable. Net: kill score 0/27 is tooling-forced, not spec weakness. `BoundaryPMFinal` (same `Node*(*lists)[2],leaves,pool` shape, plus nested `is_fresh` on `lists[index][1]`, `lists[index-1][0/1]`, `pool->next` so even heavier harness) verifies a STRONG branch-conditional `ensures` (deref nested list pointers — safe here because those nested pointers are made fresh in requires), but `ensures(0==1)` still SUCCEEDS at depth 200 AND 450 → vacuous, 0/16 kills forced.

`ExtractBitLengths(Node *chain, Node *leaves, unsigned *bitlengths)` (3 indexed pointer params; restricted to single-node chain via `requires chain->tail==0` so the `chain->tail` walk is safe without per-node freshness) directly demonstrates the wall both ways: (1) `is_fresh` on all 3 → "verified" but VACUOUS (0/12 kills, loop-body mutants at lines 332-340 all survive). (2) Swapping the two *indexed* pointers (`leaves`, `bitlengths`) to the light `r_ok`/`w_ok` predicates DOES fit depth 200 and REACHES the body (non-vacuous) — but then CBMC cannot discharge the array bounds / `assigns`-assignable checks: `r_ok`/`w_ok`, even combined with `__CPROVER_OBJECT_SIZE(p)==n` and `__CPROVER_POINTER_OFFSET(p)==0` assumptions, do NOT make the bounds checker treat the object as sized — `bitlengths[leaves[k].count]` reports "pointer outside object bounds" + "is assignable: FAILURE". Only `is_fresh` establishes a bounded object for write-through/indexed accesses, and only `is_fresh` is heavy enough to overrun depth 200. So: indexed pointers MUST be `is_fresh` (sound) → vacuous; or light predicates (fits) → unsound bounds FAILURE. No middle ground. Net 0/12 is tooling-forced.

`AddLZ77Data(lz77, lstart, lend, expected_data_size, ll_symbols, ll_lengths, d_symbols, d_lengths, bp, out, outsize)` is the heaviest seen: ~11 pointers needing memory (`lz77` + its `litlens`/`dists` arrays, four `ZOPFLI_NUM_LL`/`ZOPFLI_NUM_D` tables, `bp`, `outsize`, `out`, `*out`). A sound strong contract (is_fresh on all, forall bounding litlens/dists per literal-vs-match, ll/d_lengths in [1,15], expected_data_size==0) verifies at depth 200 but 0/40 kills — even `i<lend`→`i>lend` and the literal/match `dist==0`→`dist!=0` branch mutants survive, proving the loop body is never reached. Compounding the vacuity, it is also non-vacuously UNVERIFIABLE if reached: the callees `AddHuffmanBits`/`AddBits` are contract-replaced and their `ensures` constrain only `*bp`/`*outsize` (not buffer validity), so after the first call `*out` is havoc'd and the second sequential call's `requires is_fresh(*out, *outsize+1)` cannot be discharged. So lightening the harness to fit depth 200 would flip it to FAILURE, not kills. Net 0/40 tooling-forced.

`AddNonCompressedBlock(options, final, in, instart, inend, bp, out, outsize)` (4 pointer params needing memory: `in`, `bp`, `out`, `*out`, `outsize`; `options` is only `(void)`-cast so never deref'd, no is_fresh needed) is the same wall. A general contract (is_fresh all, `*bp<=7`, `*outsize` in [1, max_malloc/2], `inend-instart<=8`, ensures `*bp==0` and `*outsize>=old+4`) verifies at depth 200 → 0/26 kills. I also tried the OPPOSITE extreme to force reachability: minimal harness `*outsize==1` + `*bp==0` + `inend-instart<=1` (so the in-body `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` memset stays 1-2 bytes, ruling out "large memset blows depth" as the cause) PLUS byte-precise postconditions pinning every emitted byte ((*out)[1]=BFINAL, [2..5]=LEN/NLEN header, [6]=data). Still 0/26 — so it is the is_fresh/malloc *harness setup* exhausting depth 200, not the loop body's memset size. No spec change rescues kills here.

`CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall(ll_lengths, d_lengths, lz77, lstart, lend)` (same lz77+litlens/dists+ll/d_lengths shape as `AddLZ77Data`, 4 indexed pointers + lz77 struct deref = 5 is_fresh objects) is the same wall. A sound strong contract (is_fresh on all 5, `lend<=lz77->size`, forall bounding `litlens[i]<=258` and `dists[i]!=0 ==> litlens[i]>=3 && dists[i]<=32768` for callee preconds, `assigns()`, ensures `ret>=ll_lengths[256]` and `lstart==lend ==> ret==ll_lengths[256]`) verifies at depth 200 → 0/16 kills, all 16 surviving mutants are loop-guard/body RELATIONAL ops (never reached). Confirmed vacuous: added `ensures(1==0)` → SUCCESS at depth 200, but postcondition.3 FAILS at depth 600 (body finally reached). Net 0/16 tooling-forced; left the strong sound spec in place.

Bonus: at depth >=350, CBMC finds a real latent bug — `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` (macro in zopfli.h) writes through `realloc`'s return with no NULL check. See [[cbmc-treesitter-loopcontract-misparse]] and [[cbmc-aarch64-fileh-stub]].

lines: `CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall(ll_lengths, d_lengths, lz77, lstart, lend)` (same lz77+litlens/dists+ll/d_lengths shape as `AddLZ77Data`, 4 indexed pointers + lz77 struct deref = 5 is_fresh objects) is the same wall. A sound strong contract (is_fresh on all 5, `lend<=lz77->size`, forall bounding `litlens[i]<=258` and `dists[i]!=0 ==> litlens[i]>=3 && dists[i]<=32768` for callee preconds, `assigns()`, ensures `ret>=ll_lengths[256]` and `lstart==lend ==> ret==ll_lengths[256]`) verifies at depth 200 → 0/16 kills, all 16 surviving mutants are loop-guard/body RELATIONAL ops (never reached). Confirmed vacuous: added `ensures(1==0)` → SUCCESS at depth 200, but postcondition.3 FAILS at depth 600 (body finally reached). Net 0/16 tooling-forced; left the strong sound spec in place.

+`CalculateBlockSymbolSizeGivenCounts(ll_counts, d_counts, ll_lengths, d_lengths, lz77, lstart, lend)` is the HEAVIEST count-table case: its `if` branch delegates to `CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall` (so needs the full lz77+litlens+dists+forall-bounds harness) AND its `else` branch sums over four full histogram tables (so needs is_fresh on ll_counts/d_counts/ll_lengths/d_lengths too) = 7 is_fresh objects. A sound strong contract (is_fresh all 7, lstart<=lend<=lz77->size, forall litlens<=258 & dists DEFLATE bounds, assigns(), ensures ret>=ll_lengths[256]) verifies at depth 200 → 0/21 kills, all 21 surviving mutants are the if-guard relational/arith ops at line 317 and the loop bodies (never reached). Confirmed vacuous via the documented `ensures(1==0)` trick: it still "verifies" (mutation testing only runs post-success). Net 0/21 tooling-forced; left the strong sound spec in place.

Bonus: at depth >=350, CBMC finds a real latent bug — `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` (macro in zopfli.h) writes through `realloc`'s return with no NULL check. See [[cbmc-treesitter-loopcontract-misparse]] and [[cbmc-aarch64-fileh-stub]].

input.command: run-cbmc --function CalculateBlockSymbolSizeGivenCounts --file zopfli.c -I /tmp/cbmc-inc 2>&1 | grep -iE "verified successfully|kill score" | head

content.text:
## Summary

`CalculateBlockSymbolSizeGivenCounts` in `/app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c` now **verifies successfully** with a strong, sound contract:

- **`requires`**: `__CPROVER_is_fresh` on all four histogram tables (`ll_counts`, `d_counts`, `ll_lengths`, `d_lengths` — full `ZOPFLI_NUM_LL`/`ZOPFLI_NUM_D` size), on `lz77` and its `litlens`/`dists` arrays, plus `lstart <= lend <= lz77->size` and the DEFLATE `forall` bounds on `litlens`/`dists` (needed for the `else` branch's array accesses and the `if` branch's delegation to the contract-replaced `CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall`).
- **`assigns()`** (pure) and **`ensures __CPROVER_return_value >= ll_lengths[256]`** — the end symbol is always included in both branches.

**Kill score: 0/21 — this is tooling-forced, not a weak spec.** This is the heaviest instance of the documented depth-200 `is_fresh`/malloc-harness vacuity wall: it needs 7 fresh objects (vs. 5 for its already-vacuous callee `CalculateBlockSymbolSizeSmall`), so the harness setup overruns `run-cbmc`'s fixed `--depth 200` before the function body runs. I confirmed the vacuity with the documented `ensures(1 == 0)` test — it still "verified," proving the exit is never reached. All 21 surviving mutants are in the unreachable `if`-guard and loop bodies.

No sound spec can raise the kill score here without changing the tool's depth, which the project rules forbid hard-coding. I left the strong sound spec in place and recorded this confirmed instance in the existing memory note.
